title: vimeo_1191749429
displayTitle: 2026-05-20 - Governance and Administration Committee - Inquiry into Fire and Emergency New Zealand's fleet management and related issues
topic: Inquiry into Fire and Emergency New Zealand's fleet management and related issues
description: Wednesday 13 May 2026 | Governance and Administration Committee Inquiry into Fire and Emergency New Zealand's fleet management and related issues Oral Submissions Adriana de Souza [Zoom] New Zealand Firefighters Professionals Union Wellington locals Alan Collet Murray Dudfield Taxpayers' Union Ltd Ray Deacon Snow Ryan [Zoom] New Zealand Professional Firefighters Union Rotorua Local [Zoom] Chris Heath
descriptionSummary: Inquiry into Fire and Emergency New Zealand's fleet management and related issues. Oral Submissions. Adriana de Souza [Zoom]

chunks.text: Session of the hearings of the, um, inquiry into Fire and Emergency New Zealand's fleet management and related issues. And we have our first submitter online, Adriana de Souza. If you can hear me, Adriana, can you put your camera on and make sure you're unmuted, and we will get going once you have appeared. There you are.

Adriana, you have five minutes with us now, and lovely to see you, and over to you. Cool. So I made my submission because, um, I am a concerned member of the public, um, and I want to express my serious concerns about the level of resourcing available to FENZ. In particular, in relation to the reliability, availability, and the management of its appliance fleet.

Not only as a concerned member of the public, I have had, um, first-hand experience of a major fire incident in my own neighborhood back in 2024 with the Parnell Lodge fire. I lived at one of the neighboring properties and I saw the first-hand the sort of the work and effort that the FENZ team puts into the community in terms of protecting everybody and keeping everybody safe, um, and witnessed their level of service and dedication to the community after the effect as well.

I think we should be able to, in this country, be confident that when emergencies occur, that FENZ is resourced correctly to help us. Right. Have you got anything else that you wanted to add before I open up for questions, Adriana? No, that's all.

Okay. So I'll just open up and see if there's any questions from the committee. Sure. Tim? One question here from Tim Costley. Kia ora, Adriana, and it was really good reading your submission and hearing about your experience, not just with, uh, it was the Parnell fire, I think, from memory. Yes.

So I think you wrote about the follow-up and the aftermath and how engaged the FENZ staff were on the ground of the firefighters that were out there, and I think that's really encouraging to hear. When we think about the vehicles and the fleet, what is the either the biggest concern or the biggest thing that you think we should be thinking about or FENZ should be thinking about in terms of managing those appliances?

Maintenance and sort of availability of vehicles. There should be enough. We shouldn't have to have fire engines or, um, departments having to close for certain hours during the day. I know that with the FENZ one, they all came from, I think, multiple, um, departments. Sorry, the Parnell one. They all came from multiple, um, houses over, um, because the local Parnell one was closed at the time, which shouldn't have been a worry.

Okay. And, uh, and in terms of y- one of your recommendations was around long-term funding. If that meant, uh, you just let me devil's advocate this for a minute, if that's okay. If that, o- obviously, FENZ get effectively all of their funding through people like you and me paying our insurance.

Would you be okay with paying a little bit more on your levy to fund more trucks or whatever, if, if that was the only way to fund it? Would you be... What do you think the reaction would be? Personally, having first-hand experiences, I would. There's not an amount of money I wouldn't pay- Yeah... and it would be justified to me.

I think you meet these people on some of the worst days of your life, and then you think, "Great, I am more than willing to pay 5% more on my insurance levy." That's helpful. Every time they shake a bucket with me now, I, we donate. Thank you. Adriana, that appears to be all the questions.

Thank you very much and thank you for sharing your experience, even the trauma that you've been through. Um, very helpful for us to hear that. So thank you very much for making that submission. Perfect. Thanks, guys. Bye. Thank you. Right, that brings us on to New Zealand Firefighters Professional Union Wellington Locals. We've got...

Thank you. Thank you for your time with us here today. Thanks very much. And, um, are you Alan? Yes. Alan. Thank you, Alan, for making time to come and meet with us. We have 10 minutes with you, and I'm sure you know the drill. Yep. That's inclusive of questions if you want us to have that opportunity.

Okay. Thank you for the opportunity to speak today. My name is Alan Collett. I'm a career Senior Station Off- Officer within FENZ. I am not an elected NZPFU official, but I have assisted in drafting the submission from the Wellington branch of the NZPFU. I'm based in Wellington and have worked throughout the wider Wellington region, and more recently, I spent a short period of time in an operational response role in the Nelson area.I have 20 years in operational roles within FENZ and previously in New Zealand Fire Service.

This also includes involvement in a number of projects of national significance. Prior to being a firefighter, I spent just over a decade in the New Zealand Army, during which time I deployed operationally, and I also have extensive experience working in high-risk environments, spending seven years working offshore for various NGOs and commercial entities in the landmine and explosive ordnance disposal industry.

I would like to speak to three key points today: current and future needs, the response network that has been referred to within previous meetings, and the alternative tactical selection by officers as referred to in previous meetings. Current and future needs. I would like to speak to future needs.

I believe FENZ has a lack of foresight as to what its changing response landscape looks like, in particular in relation to urban aerial appliances, something that has been a topic of this inquiry. As you are aware, five aerials have been purchased to fill an immediate need.

However, although convened, a working group is yet to meet to discuss what the future aerial strategy may look like to take us into the lifespan of any new purchases. This group finally comes together next month after a five-year wait. Yes, we have purchased five medium to heavy aerials, and the push to a 45-meter reach was insightful for both Auckland and Hamilton. But how can we truly understand what capability we require from these appliances when a robust analysis of international trends, the direction of construction

design, and wider influences have not yet been analyzed? Operational procedures in relation to response to fire within multi-story buildings have not been reviewed with any significance for over 10 years. This despite multiple international incidents to learn from, behavioral changes of the public, and political influence into the changes in the construction sector.

Appliances in the type four category are already nearing end of life. We have purchased five new aerial appliances at a cost of $12.5 million and we have not fully yet analyzed what our capability needs really are in relation to a high-rise response strategy. Key pieces of work need to be completed so they inform the direction of the organization's operational response.

Projects are often underway and are being conducted with what appears to be no cohesive approach, and it is surely fiscally inefficient in the long term. The response network. You have been told that FENZ operates a structured network in response to a reported peak of 246 mechanical breakdowns in the financial year of '24 and '25.

However, what you may not be aware of are the vulnerabilities at the varying layers of that network. In the response environment, we operate in a world of incomplete information, and from the time of dispatch, the wrong or conflicting information from a 111 caller may be the start of it.

Communication centers that are under strain then dispatch appliances as required. Maybe then staffing shortages or the inability of volunteers to turn out play a part and increased drive times due to station closures and increased traffic flows delay arrival. These are some of the vulnerabilities within the network, and if we apply James Reason's Swiss cheese risk modeling, we can see how these vulnerabilities start to align and systematic failure is not far off.

For senior leaders to minimize the impact of these mechanical breakdowns is an acceptance of them and is the first sign of the drift to failure. It is the normalization of deviance, which in turn starts to undermine the other layers of the network. Rasmussen's barrier model tells us we are skirting the boundaries of acceptable performance, and within the barrier model, it is identified even a minor system disturbance or a sudden loss of operational buffer can cause a sudden jump over the edge into systematic failure.

Tactical selection by officers. I've heard it being said that FENZ officers and firefighters are trained to select alternative tactics when the appropriate resourcing is not made available due to mechanical breakdowns. Yes, we seek an alternative tactic, but often it is due to the fact we are forced into selecting inferior tactics that do not necessarily lead to the best incident outcomes.

When an incident occurs, a tactical window of opportunity opens. The longer it takes for arrival, that window starts to close. Tactical options start to become limited or are taken off the table. If the right resourcing is not available in the right timeframe, such as an aerial appliance with the right capability, these optimal tactics are no longer available to us for selection and the window continues to close.

We operate with incomplete information and we operate within constraints of time. This is the essence of studies by Gary Klein into the decision making methodologies of first responders. Senior leadership seem to have lost sight of this and as the tactical window closes, we have limited opportunities to implement aggressive life-saving tactics, as we have recently witnessed in house fires in Hamilton and Christchurch.

The economic impact on society increases with the increased property loss.Recently, FENZ added an additional measure to the annual reporting. Structure fires contained within a room of origin where suppression was required. The target has been set at 80% of the time, and they are barely achieving 61.6%.

And if we speak to the training of officers and firefighters, both career and volunteer, I am confident that we will find no distinct detail on formal learning outcomes where officers and firefighters are taught tactical selection and deployment. It is learnt through osmosis and one's personal experience.

A recent internal review of FENZ's wider training curriculum has found systematic inconsistencies, misaligned content, inconsistent assessment practices, and training facilities and learning resources that are outdated and require urgent improvement and investment. More vulnerabilities within the network.

Thank you for your time, and I'm happy to attempt to answer any questions you may have. Thank you. Thanks, Allen, for that. We've got time for a couple of questions. I've got one here. Can I just, like the reference to the James Reason model, familiar with that. In terms of the tactics, so we were told in committee back in January that if an aerial appliance breaks down, this was in response to questions about Christine, then they can change tactics and adapt and overcome.

Is there a meaningful difference in outcome if you have to change tactics? Is it consequential is what I'm asking. Yes. I'll use Lofhouse Lodge as an example. Although I did not attend the fire, I know it fairly well. The Type 5 appliance was off the run at Newtown.

It was replaced with a Type 4, temporarily, which has much more limited capability- Yes... from an upper storey rescue. When it responded to Lofhouse Lodge, it positioned itself on the southern side of the building, and then it was found it was not going to be effective there for rescues. The officer in charge had to change his tactics, and another Type 5 was requested from Thornleigh. We now know from the subsequent inquiries that people were jumping out of those windows on the southern side of the building onto the roof next door.

So we could not effect rescues on that side of the building that night. That's because it didn't have, it was such a 16 story- It either didn't have the reach or the Type 5s have a second knuckle called a fly boom, which gives them much more maneuverability. But they also told us that these days, a lot of buildings have internal systems, that it's not an external...

My understanding was that it wasn't so much about the external battle to fight the fire. Yeah. How vital are your aerial fleets? So, the building systems play a big part- Yes... in both our tactics and we can use them to our advantage, such as pressurizing stairwells and things like that. If we look at Greenfell, we had a failure in the building systems.

If we look at Lofhouse Lodge, it was an old stock building that didn't have the necessary upgrades, and there are still a lot of buildings like that within the New Zealand construction sector. Okay. Lamona. Thank you, and thank you for your submission today. I want to ask a question around training, because you have mentioned training in terms of the systematic problems, et cetera- Yeah... and the investment that's into it.

Can you comment more about how vital it is to keep the upgrade, the sustainability of both volunteer and career? Oh, it's essential. You can't minimize the importance of it. And we are still working on a training system that was designed in 2005, 2007 from the urban point of view.

With our rural colleagues, they're still on a separate training system. So part of the review was to look at what was systematically wrong, but also bring us together. What are we now? 2026, finally as one entity so that we're all on the same training system, and completing similar courses.

Okay. Just a quick, so how many training schools are being provided? Is it four? Is it one throughout the year? Or sort of does it change? It varies on your stage of your career. But if I'm a senior firefighter in the urban space and I choose not to become an officer, that is the end of my formalized training.

Everything else is done through osmosis on station as we see fit. Okay. Okay. Because we had somebody that has pulled out, I've got a couple of minutes, but I've only got a couple of minutes, but I've got two more questions, if that's okay with you, Allen. So we go to Mike and then Andy. Thank you. And my question should be quick.

Thanks for the excellent submission. Hmm. And I guess my question is, like, the issues with the fleet, is this like a symptom of a wider problem within FENZ, or is this the biggest issue we FENZ has faced? I think it's a symptom of a wider problem. Okay. In a nutshell.

Yeah. Great. Thank you. Andy. Just a quick one, and thanks for everything you do now, both now and in the past. Just in terms of the engines, the interface between the appliances and the buildings you've got. So, with the other, the highest you go is what, 45 odd meters, is that right? Of a building- And with the newer purchase it'll be 45 meters. The current Type 5s are 32.5.

Yeah. So at anything above that, you're reliant on the building systems. Yes. For most of our taller buildings now, are we in a place where the building system actually will do- So-... a lot of the work that you need to be done other than obviously you have to manually- I'm aware of a draft paper in our- How do they work? I'm aware of a draft paper in our risk reduction space- Yeah... working with the, um- A short one... industry around what we look like from 20 stories and above.

Yep. Now-After Green fell, London pushed to a 64-meter aerial. They had 32.5- Yeah... the same as us. Yeah. And they've gone to a 64-meter aerial. Okay. I'm not saying we go there, but we haven't really analyzed what those taller buildings, or super high-rises as some people call them- Yeah... what that looks like for us operationally. We haven't done the analysis.

So that's work that really needs to be done. Yes, I would say so. Okay. Thank you. She promises me it's going to be short. Yeah. Melissa. Uh, I'm just referring to five new aerial appliances at a cost of 2.5 million. Has it been delivered? Uh, they are in country, I believe, and they are currently being fitted out as much as- So it's been in country for a few years?

Uh, I think, I can't comment with any detail on that, but I think they've been in country for about a year at least. Alan, thank you very much for that. Thank you. That's been really super helpful. Brilliant. Very good for the inquiry. Thanks very much. Thank you. Thanks for coming in person as well. Thank you for your service. Okay. Murray.
Yes. Murray Duffield. Welcome, Murray.

You know the thing, we have five minutes with you. Thank you for the invitation to meet with this committee. On that understanding, I understand that my submission has been read by the members of the committee. Sure. But I'd like to add the following supplementary comments to the submission that I put in. It appears that FENS has a strong focus on the age of fire appliances more than the question around whether they're fit for purpose.

From a 2013 FENS fleet data sheet of 805 urban appliances, 500 had traveled fewer than 50,000 kilometers, which is not a large kilometer for those appliances, each appliance. Although there are some kilometer gaps in this data set, it's not a profile of a fleet suffering from excess wear, high operational demand, or systematic end-of-life failure.

What is also concerning is the negative commentary that has been aired by FENS around the age of the rural fleet that was transferred to FENS in 2017. From a survey of rural fire authorities in 2014, 392 rural fire appliances, tankers, smoke chasers, recorded at the 30th of June 2013, one-third were more than 25 years old.

Most of this rural fleet involves vehicles with low mileage, even those over 25 year old. Not all of the 392 rural vehicles were transferred to FENS in 2017. Forest Owners retained their fleet, and they've recently completed a forest fire capability study for forest owners.

From that study, forest owners had 54 forest fire 4x4 tankers, eight large water tankers, 41 smoke chasers, which are utilities with small tanks and hose reels on the back, over 300 portable forest fire pumps, 80,500 meters of forest firefighting hose, 95 heavy bulldozers in forest, and a further 258 20 or 50 ton diggers.

They have 778 trained forest firefighters and 281 fire managers and crew leaders. This resource is there to protect plantation forests, but they're also available to support FENS and the rural fire regulating role that FENS has. A further point is the general rule that 5% of the vegetation fires cost 95% of the cost of that fire suppression.

Unlike the urban environment, dedicated fire equipment is needed. Dedicated fire equipment is only a small part of the resources used to suppress vegetation fires. In the 10 years prior to 2017, the national rural fire authorities in place and the annual average breakdown of fire suppression costs for wildfires showed that 42% of the cost is on aircraft, 37% of that cost was on Department of Conservation, Forest Owners or local government.

Contractor costs such as bulldozers and diggers was 22% of the cost. Fire apart, hiring was 2%, and the fire service resources used at vegetation fires in the 10-year period prior to 2017 is 4% of that cost. What this illustrates is the majority cost of vegetation fires is on aircraft, heavy machinery, and firefighters working off-road and not urban fire trucks. Most urban trucks transport firefighters to vegetation fires and are not used for any other purpose.

History has shown that community fire brigades are very good in that first response role of suppressing small vegetation fires. But for the 5% of those wildfires that burn 95% of the area, this presents a different picture. Prior to 2017, once rural fire authority resources arrived, the volunteer brigades were able to return to their community to earn their employment.

This current situation is that FENS are requiring volunteers to spend a lot more time on vegetation fires. Happy to answer any questions. Do we have any questions? You've got one, too? Yeah. You've covered the rural thing very well. In your written submission, you also outlined the cost of some of those new stations in rural areas. But I also know you've touched on one issue that's come up a little bit, which has been around false alarms and the impact that has.

I just wondered if you wanted to touch on either the false alarms or theOr the implication of that property program with those sort of rural and low urban area, like Tenui, like Littleton stations, and the significance of that. I think probably of the two, I think the level of false alarms is concerning for New Zealand. Over 40% of all calls are false alarms. That impacts not only on the use of resources when there's probably another ways of attending to those that faulty equipment.

The call on volunteers to go to false alarms and taking that- So what's the answer? Because we're not expecting firefighters to decide if they trust this one and if they go or not. Is the answer that it's on property owners to have a better upkeep of their alarm system? Or what would you propose as the solution?

Well, my advice would be that the onus on the property owner. There's a lot of buildings out there that don't have alarm systems and I think serious consideration needs to be given to the policy that's in place for the Scotland Fire Service, where the building owner has to confirm that there is a fire as a result of that alarm. And once that's confirmed, the fire service responds to those resources. And that's the same for any homeowner or building owner that doesn't have alarm equipment, so they've got to notify the fire service to respond to that

incident. Thanks, Murray. That does bring us to the end of the time, and that was super helpful. If there are any statistics there that are new statistics, because that sounded incredibly helpful in your presentation, we would love to have that to put into our work that we're doing. I'm happy to do that.

That'd be wonderful. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you for the time. And that brings us to the New Zealand Professional Firefighters Union, Rotorua local branch. I think you're online. If you can hear us, if you can put your camera on and unmute. I think we nearly got you then.
It's tantalizingly close. Very close.
Making sure that your camera's working.
Are they there? Maybe they can unmute and, uh- If you can unmute and hear us, maybe, so we can hear you. I think it's Chris Heath that's going to be joining us.
Can we see them? They did flicker for a moment, Tom.
We'll give them a short time, and if not, we'll move to our next submitter and then we can work the technical issues out.
Looks like we're trying to get there because it keeps flickering.
Okay, have we got Snow Ryan? Snow Ryan coming on Zoom. So Snow, perhaps while we sort those technical issues out, we can go to move to you, if you can put your camera on.
Might be an hour in.

Oh, we've got an in-person. Is it Taxpayers Union? Yes, Ray, is it? Correct. Wonderful, you're here lovely and early, Ray. Thank you very much. And you know how this goes, I'm sure. We have 10 minutes with you and looking forward to your submission.

So over to you. Good afternoon, Madam Chair and members of the committee. We contend that fleet management issues are merely symptomatic of a much wider malaise within fire and emergency. We've prepared three reports over the years since the merger occurred, and we've participated in submissions on levy increases, with a focus on the efficiency of capital and operational expenditure.

But I would like to talk a bit about your main concern here, which is fleet management. Fire and Emergency have claimed that the condition of the assets it inherited in 2017 from the former rural fire authorities were much worse than first realized.

We contend that simply doesn't hold water. There was an assessment of rural fire service asset conditions made by Martin Jenkins, dated 8 May 2015, and an earlier 2013 National Rural Fire Authority survey painted the most comprehensive picture of the rural fleet and did not indicate significant issues.So, at the time of the merger in 2017, there would've been a fairly clear picture of the state of the National Fire Appliance Fleet, both those deployed in urban and rural areas.

This means, or it should mean, prior to the merger, the conditions of the rural and urban fleets would have been known and appropriate replacement plans should have been made, costed, and featured in the merger cost benefit analysis, and in the Cabinet papers and supporting documents.

It simply beggars belief that this does not appear to have been done. But what is extremely astonishing is that FENZ did not have detailed and comprehensive asset management plans until 2024 and '25. AMP is the most basic tools for any asset intensive operation, such as FENZ.

Why was this not an immediate priority? Having worked in electricity related issues for some years, I fully understand just how important AMPs are in every business, and I know it's been a focus of this government as well. Fire and Emergency submission focuses heavily on replacing vehicles beyond their target age.

Isn't a better metric one based on condition assessment? That's what a comprehensive asset management plan can provide. It should be possible to use some assets safely beyond their target age if they have been well-maintained, and say, traveled few kilometers.

FENZ is clearly doing this already, so why not formalize it? And I note that they are developed this as an ongoing work, work they had, and so maybe they are actually doing that. I just want to talk briefly about property. Why is the cost of constructing rural stations skyrocketed?

Why do some include more space and amenities than strictly necessary? Why can the Australians construct rural stations far more cheaply? I realize there are cost differences, but it seems astonishing the explosion in the cost in recent years. Now, the big issue for us is governance.

The 2017 merger was predicated on the promise of savings. Not only did those savings not occur, but costs also blew out and continued to rise rapidly until Minister Van Velden demanded savings. The excuse that the assets inherited from the rural fire authorities were worse than expected doesn't wash when reviews of their conditions were carried out pre-merger.

The failures to deliver the promised savings, would the merger actually have even gone ahead had the actual costs that have been incurred subsequent to merger been known at the time? I very much doubt it. Failures to control expenditure, failure to efficiently construct rural fire stations, failure to efficiently manage assets all suggest a major failure of governance by the board and Department of Internal Affairs.

They simply failed to hold management to account for their failure to effectively manage assets and stewardship of levy payer funds. The Taxpayers Union is aware that this committee has, in the past, asked some of these questions of FENZ and did not always get satisfactory answers.

This is actually quite concerning, that they can't answer straight questions. I seem to remember a previous chair appearing quite exasperated at not getting straight answers to straight questions. For all these reasons, this is why this committee needs to expand its investigation well beyond the fleet issues, as they are simply a symptom of a wider malaise. There needs to be a full investigation concerning the costs and benefits of the merger, a proper post-merger review needs to be started as soon as possible. But I would like to end on a positive note.

Minister Van Velden deserves thanks for appointing Mr. Ravin Jesuranam to chair the Board of FENZ. His long experience with asset management is a skill that is clearly missing. The Taxpayers' Union has little doubt that he will improve the efficiency of all that Fire and Emergency does in fairly short order. Thank you for your time.

Thanks. Thanks, Ray. That's super helpful. Now I'll open to the committee for any questions. We've got about four minutes. Sure. Ta. You referred to the cost of either rural or smaller stations and the increase in that. That's just something we've heard and that we've seen in a few recent submissions.

Do you want to just elaborate on that? And that obviously has an impact. Your premise is that the lack of savings and the blowing out of some costs has led to not enough going into fleet. Can you just explain for us a bit about those smaller stations? The issue with the cost increases in the property portfolio is, does that reflect efficient use of levy payer funds?

It's not so much that if they're paying too much, then there's not enough going into the fleet, for example. In our last report, we made the point that... Or actually, in our last submission, on levy increases, that there appears to be a cost plus attitude within Fire and EmergencyThat there's-- And in fact, in the regulatory impact statement, the Department of Internal Affairs actually questioned them.

Questioned, "What efficiencies have you tried to find before you've come to the Crown to ask for an increase in the fire service levy?" And the Auditor General also has raised concerns in the past about some of their practices. So there is a general, not only at our level, but at a clearly high level within government as well.

Thank you. Andy. Going to the, thanks, the issue you raised about the cost of fire stations having skyrocketed. One of the things they told us is that where their money was being spent in terms of capital expenditure, 40% was going on property, 20% on fleet, 20 on IT, and 20 on other equipment.

I think we were sort of raising our eyebrows at all of those, probably particularly the property one. But any comments on that? Just seems an extraordinarily high proportion of what they're spending in terms of the capital. I think the issue is, if their capital spend on property is inefficient, then as a proportion of total expenditure- It's going to rise... it would come down. Sorry, it's gone up.

Yeah. So that, again, is maybe one of those little marker posts that tell you that there's something not right here. Mm-hmm. Okay. Thank you. And question from Lamona. Yes, just very quickly, thank you for your submission and also your comments. Just around the asset management plans, because you've got comments in relation to vehicles and equipment in the final concluding. Can you just extrapolate a bit more on that very point?

Sorry, I've missed what you're after. Yeah. So you've explained here your concluding comments in your written submission- Sorry, yes... which is helpful. In terms of asset management plans, because we've had a number of submissions which have pointed to asset management plans in terms of what has been proposed and what has been delivered.

I just wanted you to add anything to that. Right. Okay. I would actually love to see what they've done. And we haven't got that yet. This still is, I understand, a work in process. I think the big issue, the surprise for me, was reading their last annual report for 2024/25 and finding out that they had only just started to develop these.

Sorry. And- Well, they're ahead of Education and Health. Yeah. Yeah. Well, again, it's an issue that Minister Bishop raised some time ago. Yeah. And it is really important, and I read some material very recently which said New Zealand used to be at the top of the world in this sort of area, and now we've fallen to the bottom.

Mm. Basically, I think public and private sectors have just taken their eye off the ball here, and it needs to go back, because it all goes back to financial efficiency. Look, thank you, Ray. That's super helpful, and that has brought us directly to the end of the time. So thank you very much for that.

Great. Thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you for your time. Yes, thank you. And that brings us back. Have we got Chris there from the Rotorua Local NZPFU? Chris, hey. It's just a matter of getting your camera, turning your camera on.
Snow instead. Oh, we might try Snow instead, I hear. So Snow, if you're there, can you turn your camera on, please?

Here we go. Oh, yes. Here we go, Snow. We need to unmute him. Just need to unmute there, Snow. And we're all set to go. Great. We've got five- Sorry about that. All good. Just one note on why we couldn't connect, is going through the browser, and it didn't give the correct permissions. So that's probably why we might be having problems with Chris.

Okay, thank you for that insight. Anyway, good afternoon. Right. We've got five minutes with you. Over to you. Wonderful. Good afternoon, and thank you for the opportunity to speak today. My name is Snow Ryan, and I'm speaking as a former firefighter and volunteer controller of a rural fire station.

My perspective comes from direct operational experience within New Zealand's fire and emergency response environment. I want to begin by saying that I support this inquiry into fire and emergency fleet management and related issues. Fleet reliability matters enormously.

Appliances that are unavailable, aging, unsuitable, or improperly maintained directly affect emergency response capability, the safety of frontline personnel, and public safety more broadly. So I believe this inquiry is both necessary and worthwhile.However, my core concern is that fleet management cannot be viewed in isolation from the wider emergency response system.

Operational readiness depends on multiple interconnected parts functioning together, such as equipment, staffing, training, communications, coordination, leadership, and organizational support. A functioning appliance alone does not create an effective emergency response if the wider system surrounding it is under strain.

Based on both operational experience and ongoing public reporting, there appear to be broader systemic pressures affecting multiple parts of Fire and Emergency New Zealand. These concerns are not confined to one area of the organization. These are not theoretical concerns.

In a single recent week, the NZPFU publicly documented an appliance breaking down en route to a CBD emergency, a station closing due to staffing failure, 111 calls diverted to police while leadership were overseas, and one of the country's busiest medical response stations left without a crew at shift changeover.

Incidents like these are reported with regularity. They reflect simultaneous failures across equipment, staffing, coordination, not isolated faults in one part of the system. They appear to affect career and volunteer personnel, urban and rural fire stations, frontline operations, and organizational coordination structures.

Concerns that continue to emerge publicly include staffing and retention pressures, operational fatigue, training access and consistency, support for volunteer brigades, communication and coordination pressures, and wider confidence in organizational management and responsiveness.

In many communities, especially regional and rural areas, volunteers remain a critical part of frontline resilience. But this should not be framed as division between career and volunteer personnel. Both parts of the system are essential, interdependent, and currently appear to be facing strain in different ways.

My concern is ultimately about system readiness as a whole. I believe there is value in examining whether current structures, funding allocation, support systems, and organizational leadership settings are fully aligned with operational realities across the country.

My recommendation to the committee is either to broaden the scope of this inquiry or to recommend a complementary system-wide review examining staffing, retention, training capacity, volunteer sustainability, operational readiness, communication system, and organizational culture across Fire and Emergency New Zealand.

Because ultimately, fleet capability is only one component of emergency readiness. A resilient emergency response system depends on alignment between equipment, people, leadership, training, coordination, and organizational support. Without examining the wider system, there is a risk that the inquiry identifies visible symptoms while deeper structural pressures remain unresolved.
